data.history: The Al Attiyah family's prominence in Qatar is extensively documented through their founding and leadership of Al Sadd Sports Club in 1969. Ali bin Hamad Al-Attiyah, born and raised in the Al Sadd district of Doha, established the club after consulting with Jassim bin Hamad Al Thani, who was minister of Youth and Sports at the time. The family's tribal affiliation with Banu Tamim is confirmed through multiple sources, with Khalid bin Mohammad Al Attiyah's biography explicitly stating that 'His family belongs to the Banu Tamim tribe to which the ruling family of Qatar, the House of Thani, also belong.' The Al Attiyah family has maintained significant influence in Qatar's political and business landscape. Abdullah bin Hamad Al Attiyah served as Qatar's minister of energy and industry from 1992-2011, later becoming deputy prime minister and head of the Emir's court. Khalid bin Mohammad Al Attiyah held multiple high-ranking positions including minister of foreign affairs (2013-2016) and minister of state for defense affairs (2016-2024). The family has also produced notable sports figures like rally champion Nasser Al-Attiyah, who is described as 'a distant relative of Tamim bin Hamad Al Thani, Emir of Qatar.' Their business interests span multiple sectors, with Abdullah Al Attiyah serving as chairman and managing director of Qatar Petroleum and holding directorships in Gulf Helicopters Corporation and Gulf Airways Corporation. Abdul Rahman bin Hamad Al Attiyah served as the fourth secretary general of the Gulf Cooperation Council (GCC) from 2002 to 2011, representing the family's regional diplomatic influence. The family has also intermarried with the ruling Al Thani family, with Sheikh Abdullah bin Hamad Al Thani (Deputy Emir of Qatar) married to Sheikha Al Maha bint Muhammad Al Attiyah in 2012.
data.description: The Al Attiyah family is a prominent Qatari family belonging to the Banu Tamim tribe, known for their significant contributions to Qatar's sports, politics, energy sector, and diplomacy. They founded Al Sadd Sports Club in 1969 and have produced multiple government ministers and business leaders.
data.originStory: The Al Attiyah family belongs to the Banu Tamim tribe, the same tribal confederation as Qatar's ruling Al Thani family. Their presence in Qatar is well-established by the 20th century, with family members taking leadership roles in various sectors including sports, government, and business.
data.modernStatus: The family maintains high-level positions in Qatar's government and business sectors, with members serving as ministers, diplomats, and corporate leaders. They continue to lead Al Sadd Sports Club, Qatar's most successful football club with 64 official championships.
data.nameEtymology: The name derives from 'Atiyya' meaning 'gift', reflecting their traditional role as generous tribal leaders.

data.history: Based on the extensive research provided, there is no connection between the Al Mu'ammar family of the GCC region and the Libyan political leader Muammar Gaddafi (c. 1942-2011). The research focuses exclusively on Gaddafi, who belonged to the Qadhadhfa tribe of Libya - described as 'a small, relatively uninfluential tribe' that was 'Arab in heritage' from the deserts of Tripolitania in western Libya. Gaddafi was born near Qasr Abu Hadi, outside Sirte, to a poor Bedouin family where his father Mohammad Abdul Salam bin Hamed bin Mohammad worked as a goat and camel herder. The family belonged to traditional nomadic Bedouin culture of Libya's desert regions, with no documented connections to the Arabian Peninsula's tribal confederations or the Gulf region. The research provides no evidence of any familial, tribal, geographical, or historical connections between Gaddafi's Libyan Qadhadhfa lineage and any Al Mu'ammar family originating from the Banu Tamim confederation in the Arabian Peninsula. The name similarity appears to be purely coincidental, as the research establishes no links between these distinct regional and tribal contexts. Without additional research specifically focusing on the GCC-based Al Mu'ammar family and their Banu Tamim origins, no verified historical narrative can be constructed about this family based on the provided materials.

data.history: The Al Sabhan are a traditional Arabian family with deep historical roots in the Anaza tribal confederation, who emerged as a powerful political dynasty during the late 19th and early 20th centuries. They played crucial roles in Arabian Peninsula politics, particularly through their strategic alliance with the Rashidi emirate of Ha'il. Hamoud bin Sabhan Al Sabhan served as regent for the young Emir Saud bin Abdulaziz Al Rashid from 1908-1909, effectively ruling the emirate during this critical period. The family's political influence was further cemented through strategic marriages, with Moudi bint Sabhan Al Sabhan marrying Abdulaziz bin Mutaib Al Rashid, the seventh Rashidi emir. Another notable family member, Zamil bin Salem Al Sabhan, succeeded Hamoud as regent and ruled until 1914 when he was assassinated following disputes over foreign policy, particularly regarding relations with Ibn Saud. The family produced influential female leaders, including Fatima bint Zamel Al Sabhan, who managed the treasury and wielded significant political influence during the Rashidi period, particularly noted for her opposition to the Saudis and Wahhabism. After the fall of the Rashidi dynasty, several Al Sabhan women married into the Saudi royal family, including Norah bint Hammud Al Sabhan who became Ibn Saud's eighth wife, and Nura bint Sabhan who married King Abdulaziz after her first husband Muhammad bin Talal was imprisoned. In modern times, the family has successfully transitioned into diplomatic and governmental roles, exemplified by Thamer bin Sabhan Al-Sabhan, who has served as Saudi Arabia's Minister of State for Gulf Affairs since 2016 and was notably the first Saudi ambassador to Iraq in over 25 years following the restoration of diplomatic ties in 2015.

data.modernStatus: Currently represented in high-level Saudi government positions, with Thamer bin Sabhan Al-Sabhan serving as Minister of State for Gulf Affairs since 2016 and former ambassador to Iraq

data.history: The Al-Ma'awdah family was among the Arab family clans that moved from Qatar to Bahrain following the Bani Utbah's conquest of the islands in 1783. According to the research, after the Bani Utbah gained control of Bahrain through their defeat of Nasr Al-Madhkur at the 1782 Battle of Zubarah, different Arab family clans and tribes from Qatar settled on the islands. The family's migration from Qatar to Bahrain was part of the broader movement of Utbah-affiliated clans who had established themselves in the Zubarah area of Qatar before expanding their influence to Bahrain. The research indicates this settlement occurred sometime after 1783, when Al-Madhkur lost control of Bahrain following his defeat by the Bani Utbah clan and allied tribes. The Al-Ma'awdah were specifically listed among the families that moved to settle in Bahrain after the fall of Nasr Al-Madhkur of Bushehr, alongside other prominent families including the House of Khalifa, Al-Buainain, Al-Fadhil, Al-Kuwari, Al-Mannai, Al-Noaimi, Al-Rumaihi, Al-Sulaiti, Al-Sadah, and Al-Thawadi.

data.history: The Almuhannada family is an offshoot of the Bani Hajer, which is itself part of the larger Qahtan tribal confederation. The Qahtan tribe is composed of three main tribes: Sanhan, Junb, and Rufaida, with members distributed across Yemen, Saudi Arabia, Oman, Qatar, Iraq, Kuwait, and the United Arab Emirates. Historically, the Qahtan tribe settled in the Najd region known as Al-Quwai'iyah and expanded its influence in southern Najd, becoming one of the powerful tribes of Najd before eventually returning to its original home in the south. In Bahrain specifically, Qahtanis represent a small number compared to other Gulf countries, but some members have taken prominent public roles. The Almuhannada family, as a Hajri offshoot, has maintained a public presence in Bahrain, with former Member of Parliament Hamad Almuhannadi serving as a notable political figure representing the family in Bahrain's legislative body. This demonstrates the family's transition from their tribal origins to active participation in modern Gulf political systems.

data.history: The research reveals a fundamental error in the existing data. The term 'Jarrahids' refers specifically to the Banū al-Jarrāḥ (Arabic: بنو الجرَّاح), an ancient Arab dynasty that ruled Palestine and controlled parts of Transjordan and northern Arabia during the late 10th and early 11th centuries CE, not a modern GCC family. The Jarrahids were the ruling clan of the Tayy tribe, one of three powerful tribes of Syria at the time, alongside Kalb and Kilab. They first emerged as allies of the Qarmatians and rose to prominence under their chieftain Mufarrij ibn Daghfal ibn al-Jarrah, who secured the governorship of Palestine from the Fatimid Caliphate in 973 CE as reward for military services. The dynasty intermittently controlled territory in Palestine, the Balqa plain east of the Jordan River, the Sharat mountains, and the north Arabian mountain ranges of Jabal Aja and Jabal Salma between 973-1041 CE. Under Hassan ibn Mufarrij, the Jarrahids formed an unprecedented alliance with the Kalb and Kilab tribes that dominated Syria until their defeat by the Fatimids in 1029 CE. The dynasty's political influence waned significantly after 1041, with only scattered mentions of later chieftains like Hazim ibn Ali and Humayd ibn Mahmud in the 1060s. Fadl ibn Rabi'ah became the progenitor of the Al Fadl dynasty, whose emirs came to dominate the Bedouin tribes of the steppe until the 18th century. This medieval Jarrahid dynasty has no documented connection to any modern GCC family bearing a similar name, making the existing data historically inaccurate.
data.description: The Jarrahids were a medieval Arab dynasty of the Tayy tribe that ruled Palestine and parts of the Levant between the 10th and 11th centuries CE. This historical dynasty has no verified connection to any contemporary GCC family.
data.originStory: The Jarrahids originated as the ruling clan of the Tayy tribe and initially controlled fortresses in the Sharat mountains. They emerged in Muslim historical sources as allies of the Qarmatians in the late 10th century CE.

data.history: The Al Ansari family traces its ancient lineage to the Ansar of Medina, specifically to the companion Abu Ayyub al-Ansari (Khalid ibn Zayd ibn Kulayb ibn Tha'laba) from the Banu Najjar tribe of the Khazraj. According to Islamic historical sources, Abu Ayyub al-Ansari was a close companion of Prophet Muhammad who hosted him after the Hijra in 622 CE and became the waqif (endower) of Al-Masjid an-Nabawi by purchasing the land on behalf of the Prophet. Two of Abu Ayyub's sons, Khalid and Ayyub, remained in the Arabian Peninsula and their descendants later migrated to Gulf countries including Qatar and Bahrain. His other son, Abu Mansur Ummatul Ansari, took part in the Muslim conquest of Khorasan and settled in Herat, with his family remaining there until 1526 when they migrated to the Indian subcontinent with Mughal emperor Babur. The distinguished Ansari lineage continued through Islamic history, with notable scholars like the 15th-16th century Zakariya al-Ansari, who served as chief judge in Egypt. In modern times, the Ansari name appears in various contexts including diplomatic service, as evidenced by Mohammad Hamid Ansari who served as India's Vice President and had extensive diplomatic experience in Gulf countries including the UAE. The family's merchant tradition aligns with the historical pattern of Ansari descendants establishing trading networks across the Islamic world, building business empires spanning money exchange, trading, and financial services, particularly through establishing Al Ansari Exchange, one of the Middle East's largest remittance companies.
data.description: The Al Ansari family is a prominent merchant family in the GCC region, tracing their lineage to the Ansar of Medina and known for building a business empire spanning money exchange, trading, and financial services. They are particularly renowned for establishing Al Ansari Exchange, one of the Middle East's largest remittance companies.
data.originStory: The Al Ansari family derives its name and lineage from the Ansar (supporters) of Medina who aided Prophet Muhammad during the Hijra in 622 CE, specifically from Abu Ayyub al-Ansari who hosted the Prophet and purchased the land for Al-Masjid an-Nabawi. They migrated from the Arabian Peninsula to the Gulf region, establishing themselves as merchants and traders.

data.history: The Al Rumaithi family's contemporary prominence in UAE government is documented through several high-ranking officials who have served in key positions since the country's formation. Saeed Khalifa Humeid Al Rumaithi emerged as a notable government figure, serving as secretary of the Ministry of Labor in Abu Dhabi for approximately two decades before resigning in late 2004. His career trajectory included earlier work in the Abu Dhabi Culture and Heritage Department, demonstrating the family's involvement in both cultural preservation and labor administration during the UAE's formative years. Following his government service, Saeed Khalifa Humeid Al Rumaithi transitioned into charitable and business leadership roles, serving on the Zayed Charitable Foundation Board of Trustees and the GIC Board of Directors, while also founding the Care Institute. The family's continued influence in UAE institutions is exemplified by Major General Hamidi al-Rumaithi, who serves as commander of the UAE Navy as of 2026, representing the family's significant role in the country's defense establishment. This naval leadership position places the Al Rumaithi family among the key military families responsible for the UAE's maritime security and regional defense capabilities.

data.history: The bin Laden family traces its origins to Awad bin Laden from the village of al-Rubat in Wadi Doan of the Tarim Valley, Hadramout governorate, Yemen. The family belongs to the Kindah tribe, a Kindite Hadrami tribal lineage from Yemen's eastern region. The family patriarch Mohammed bin Awad bin Laden (1908-1967) emigrated from Hadramout to Saudi Arabia prior to World War I, initially working as a porter in Jeddah and later as a bricklayer with Aramco before starting his own construction business in 1931. Mohammed strategically cultivated close relationships with the Saudi royal family, particularly by constructing a ramp at King Abdulaziz's Jeddah palace to help the aging monarch move between floors. His remarkable business acumen, reliability, and willingness to offer the lowest bids on construction contracts led him to become a multi-millionaire by the 1940s. Major breakthroughs included building a palace for future King Saud in 1948 and the crucial Jeddah-Medina road in the early 1950s. King Faisal eventually issued a royal decree awarding Mohammed exclusive rights to all future construction projects, leading to assets exceeding $5 billion and making him the wealthiest non-royal Saudi. The company held exclusive rights to mosque construction across Saudi Arabia and several Arab countries, including restoration work at Jerusalem's Al-Aqsa Mosque until 1967. Mohammed married 22 times and fathered 52-54 children, including his 17th child Osama bin Laden, born to his Syrian wife Hamida al-Attas (later Alia Ghanem). After Mohammed's death in a 1967 plane crash in Usran, 'Asir Province, his eldest son Salem took over the family business until his own death in a 1988 ultralight aircraft accident in Texas. Bakr bin Laden then assumed leadership of what became the Saudi Binladin Group. The family publicly disowned Osama in 1994 due to his terrorist activities, and their construction empire continues as one of the Middle East's largest firms. In 2009, Forbes listed the bin Laden family as the 5th wealthiest Saudi family with a reported net worth of $7 billion. Following Bakr's detention in Saudi Arabia's corruption crackdown in 2018, the family transferred their stake to the government, effectively nationalizing the company.

Mohammed bin Awad bin Laden emigrated from this region to Saudi Arabia before World War I, establishing the family's presence in the Kingdom.

data.history:
The Al Farooq (Arabic: فاروق) family are a Sunni Muslim business family originally from Saudi Arabia who later settled in Bastak in the Hormozgan province of southern Iran before migrating to Dubai. They belong to the Al Ansar tribe and are among the first of UAE's business and aristocratic families. The family led by Al Hajj Mohammed Aqil was part of the first wave of migration from Bastak which started in 1899 due to the introduction of customs tax by the Persian government under the Qajar dynasty which led to reduced profits for merchants. Political instability in 1909 and a flu epidemic in 1918 saw the heads of most merchant families from Bastak relocate to the Persian Gulf region, Karachi and the Indian Ocean regions.

After Al Hajj Mohammed Aqil died, his sons Mohammed Farooq (known as 'The Great Farooq') and Ahmed Al Arshi took over as heads of the family, expanding the business internationally and mainly trading in diamonds, pearls and precious stones. Mohammed Farooq is remembered as the most famous pearl merchant of Lengeh, Bastak and the Persian Gulf. His generosity and hospitality earned him respect and a reputable status amongst Gulf royalty, including close ties with Sheikh Mohammed Bin Saqer Al Qasimi (former ruler of Sharjah and Ras Al Khaimah) and Sheikh Saeed Bin Maktoum Al Maktoum (former ruler of Dubai).

After Mohammed Farooq died, his son Abdulkarim Farooq became the heir to the family and was an advisor to the former rulers across the Persian Gulf. He owned a general trading establishment in Al Bastakiya, Dubai known as London Store which specialized in importing quality brands directly from London, UK. He was regarded as the 'wealthiest man in Dubai and owned symbols of affluence which included a yacht'. The family has a notable history of female achievement - Faiqa Farooq, daughter of The Great Farooq, was the Arab world's first female ambassador of the United Nations, born in Paris in 1929.

The Al Farooq family has maintained a strong tradition of social responsibility and philanthropy. The Bait (house) Farooq in Bahrain played host to many events that contributed to society, including a mass meeting in 1948 that provided initial impetus for Bahrain's support for Palestine. Today, their generosity is evident through various landmarks including mosques and the Farooq House (part of the Dubai creek heritage in Al Bastakiya), and a museum in Bandar Lengeh. As of 2014, the majority of family members reside in the UAE, Bahrain, Kuwait, the United States and Canada. The Farooq Al Arshi family are first cousins of the Al Olema family (also known as Sheikh Sultan Al Ulama, the Fikree, and Al Khaldi family).
data.description: A Sunni Muslim business family originally from Saudi Arabia, later based in Bastak, Iran before migrating to Dubai starting in 1899. They were among the first UAE business and aristocratic families, known for pearl trading and maintaining close ties with Gulf royalty.
data.originStory: Originally from Saudi Arabia belonging to the Al Ansar tribe, the family settled in Bastak in the Hormozgan province of southern Iran before eventually migrating to Dubai starting in 1899 due to customs taxation and political instability under the Persian Qajar dynasty.
data.modernStatus: The rapidly expanding family business is now described as a truly global brand with members residing primarily in the UAE, Bahrain, Kuwait, the United States and Canada. Current business leadership includes Mohammed Farooq Abdulkarim Farooq, Essa Farooq Abdulkarim Farooq and Rashid Farooq.
data.nameEtymology: Al-Fārūq literally means 'the one who distinguishes between right and wrong' in Arabic. The family name is often spelled 'Farooq', 'Faruk' or 'Faruq' in English, however in Arabic and Persian the spelling is فاروق.

data.history: The Al Naghi family traces its origins to Persian scholarly lineage, specifically descended from the prominent Iranian philosopher Mir Damad (c. 1561-1631/1632). Mir Damad, also known as Seyyed Mir Mohammad Baqer Esterabadi, was a leading Twelver Shia philosopher, founder of the School of Isfahan, and architect of the Shah Mosque in Isfahan. He was called 'Mir Damad' (Groom of the King) because he married Shah Abbas's daughter. The family derives its name from Mir Damad's son, Seyed Ali Naghi Astarabadi, with the lineage continuing through his grandson Sayyid Mahdi bin Sayyid Ali Naqi. The research confirms that the family name 'Naghi' appears to derive from this Ali Naghi lineage. In the modern era, the Al Naghi name has evolved from its Persian scholarly roots into contemporary business ventures. The family's presence in the automotive industry is represented through Mohamed Yousuf Naghi Motors in Saudi Arabia, which became part of a significant business consortium in 2022. This consortium, comprising BMW importers and distributors from Kuwait, Saudi Arabia, and local Egyptian investors, acquired the Bavarian Auto Group in Egypt, renamed it as the Global Auto Group, and signed new BMW and MINI assembly agreements. This transition from 17th-century Persian philosophical traditions to modern automotive business represents a remarkable evolution across centuries, maintaining the family's prominence while adapting to contemporary commercial opportunities.

data.history: The Al Fakieh family is a prominent Saudi Arabian business family with established commercial operations centered in Jeddah and Mecca, though their specific ethnic or tribal origins are not documented in available sources. The family built their commercial empire through diversified business ventures, beginning with poultry farming operations that would later supply their restaurant chain. In 1989, Abdul Rahman Fakieh founded Al Tazaj restaurant chain in Mecca, specializing in flame-grilled chicken prepared with proprietary sauces and sourcing chickens from their own Fakieh Poultry Farms. The family expanded beyond food services into entertainment through the Tarfeeh Fakieh Group, which owns and operates Al-Shallal Theme Park in Jeddah that officially opened on November 25, 2004. By 2001, Tarfeeh Fakieh Group had grown to become the 34th largest company in Saudi Arabia with over $480 million in assets and more than 6,000 employees, demonstrating the family's successful business diversification strategy. The family gained significant political prominence through Adel Fakeih, who served in multiple high-profile government positions including Mayor of Jeddah (2005-2010), Minister of Labor (2010-2015), Minister of Health (2014), and Minister of Economy and Planning (2015-2017), where he played a key role in developing Saudi Arabia's Vision 2030 economic transformation plan. However, the family's political influence was significantly disrupted in November 2017 when both Adel Fakeih and businessman Abdulrahman Fakieh were arrested during the Saudi Arabian anti-corruption purge, marking a dramatic shift in their public standing and political involvement.
data.description: Prominent Saudi Arabian business family based in Jeddah and Mecca, known for their diversified commercial empire including Al Tazaj restaurant chain, Fakieh Poultry Farms, and Al-Shallal Theme Park, who gained significant political influence through former minister Adel Fakeih before being impacted by the 2017 anti-corruption purge.

data.history: The Al Wazzan family traces its origins to the Najd region of the Arabian Peninsula, with lineage connecting to the Banu Tamim tribe, before establishing themselves as prominent merchants in Kuwait. The family name al-Wazzan has historical significance across the Arab world, notably appearing in the 16th century with al-Ḥasan ibn Muḥammad ibn Aḥmad al-Wazzān (c. 1494-1554), known as Leo Africanus, though direct family connections between these lineages cannot be established from available research. Leo Africanus was born in Granada and moved to Fez, becoming a renowned geographer and diplomat who wrote the influential 'Description of Africa' in 1526 after converting to Christianity in Rome. The Al Wazzan family's establishment in Kuwait reflects the broader pattern of Najdi families creating successful commercial enterprises in the Gulf region, rising to significant influence in Kuwaiti merchant society. In contemporary times, the family has diversified beyond traditional commerce into creative industries. Abdullah Al-Wazzan, born in 1990 in Kuwait City, represents a new generation pursuing careers in architecture, filmmaking, and entrepreneurship. His achievements include creating 'Falafel Cart' (2019), which became Kuwait's first film officially selected at the Hiroshima International Animation Festival and the first Kuwaiti short film submitted to the Academy Awards, marking a significant milestone in Kuwaiti cinema history.

data.history: The Al Khater (آل خاطر) is a prestigious Arabian Peninsula family belonging to Al Buainain of Banu Tamim, with origins tracing to Bani Abdullah bin Darem, a branch of Bani Hanzala from Banu Tamim. Their ancestral roots trace back to Hotat Bani Tamim, from where they migrated to settle in an area called 'Aynain' (two springs), giving the tribe its name. The family expanded across multiple Arabian Peninsula countries including Saudi Arabia, Qatar, and Bahrain, where they established themselves as influential builders and political allies. They are credited with founding two major Arabian Peninsula cities: Jubail in Saudi Arabia and Al Wakrah, the former capital of Qatar. The family developed strong political and marital ties with the ruling families of the Gulf states through strategic alliances and intermarriages. Their relationship with Al Saud began when Mohammed bin Hassan Al Khater provided refuge to Abdul Rahman ibn Faisal, the last ruler of the Second Saudi State, in his mosque in Bahrain during his exile. In Saudi Arabia, Sheikh Mohammed bin Ali Al Khater, ruler of Jubail, joined forces with Abdul-Aziz bin Saud in the unification wars, participating in battles such as Kinzan in 1915. With Al Khalifa of Bahrain, the family maintained strong ties through Sheikh Mohammed bin Hassan's extensive agricultural holdings near Bahrain International Airport and his role in prisoner exchanges. The strongest family bonds were forged with Al Thani through multiple strategic marriages, including Sheikh Mohammed bin Hassan's marriage to Sheikha Sabika, daughter of Sheikh Qassim bin Mohammed Al Thani, establishing one of the strongest family alliances in Qatar.
data.description: A prestigious Arabian Peninsula family from Banu Tamim who founded Jubail and Al Wakrah, maintaining strong political and marital ties with the ruling families of Saudi Arabia, Qatar, and Bahrain. The family has produced numerous diplomats, government officials, and business leaders across the Gulf states.

data.modernStatus: The family continues to hold prominent positions across Gulf governments, with members serving as ambassadors, ministers, and senior officials. Notable contemporary figures include Lolwah bint Rashed Al-Khater, Qatar's Minister of Education and Higher Education, and various family members serving in diplomatic and governmental roles across Qatar, Saudi Arabia, and Bahrain.

data.history: Al-'Ababsah (العبابسة) is a clan within the Manasir tribe of Northern Sudan, not to be confused with Arabian Peninsula tribes of similar names. They are classified among the Riverain Manasir (Manasir al-Nil) who inhabit the Fourth Cataract region of the Nile in Northern Sudan, in an area called Dar al-Manasir. The al-'Ababsah clan is listed alongside other Riverain Manasir clans including al-Wahabab, al-Suleimaniyah, al-Kabanah, al-Diqeisab, al-Hamsab, al-Ga'al, al-Farei'ab, al-Hamdatiab, and al-'Amasib. According to Manasir oral tradition, they trace their lineage to Mansur, who is claimed to belong to the line of descendants of al-'Abbas, the uncle of Prophet Muhammad, though multiple conflicting origin stories exist within the broader Manasir community. Critical research suggests that the current Manasir community, including al-'Ababsah, represents a voluntary amalgamation over centuries between indigenous mostly Nubian groups, descendants of emigrating Arabs, and recruited tribal minorities. The Manasir are described as an indigenous Nile culture who adopted Islam and speak Arabic as their first language, similar to their neighboring tribes the Rubatab and Shaiqiyah. As of 1993, the total Manasir population was approximately 30,000 residents with an additional 17,000 non-resident members. The al-'Ababsah clan, like other Riverain Manasir, pursues small-scale agriculture on alluvial soils near the Nile, with dates being their most important cash crop sold on the national market.

data.history:
Ahl al-Sumada is one of the major branches of the Al-Dhafeer tribe, which descends from Tayy through Banu Lam according to genealogical sources including Muhammad Amin al-Baghdadi's 'Gold Alloys in the Knowledge of Arab Tribes' and Hamad al-Jassir's 'Jamharat Ansab al-Osar in Najd'. The Al-Dhafeer tribe's ancestral origins trace back to areas near Medina, specifically from a place called al-Dhaghn opposite Medina, before migrating through various stages across the Arabian Peninsula.

As part of Al-Dhafeer, Ahl al-Sumada comprises seven distinct sub-branches: Al-Jawasim, Al-Thira'an, Al-Ma'alem, Al-Ma'adin, Al-Oraif, Al-'Aljanat, and Al-Askar. The tribe followed the typical Bedouin migration pattern from their original settlements east of Medina to central Najd, then to north Najd and north Bahrain (Eastern Arabia), eventually encompassing the desert regions of south Iraq, north Saudi Arabia, and north Kuwait. The Al-Dhafeer were known for their nomadic lifestyle and raiding activities, earning the nickname 'People of flowing hair and camel caravans' due to their practice of raiding alongside women and children.

Historically, Ahl al-Sumada participated in significant military engagements as part of the Al-Dhafeer confederation. In 1782, fighters from the Dhafeer tribe, including members of Ahl al-Sumada, participated in the Battle of Zubarah, helping the House of Khalifa defeat Persian forces in Bahrain. The tribe later achieved a notable victory at the Battle of Qaysar in 1924, where they defeated Ikhwan forces from the Mutair tribe, killing 72 fighters including the prominent leader Haif bin Shuqair al-Duwaish.

Around 1926, the tribal dynamics changed when Ibn Saud allowed the Saudi branch of the Al-Dhafeer tribe, including Ahl al-Sumada, to settle permanently after securing the roads in his expanding kingdom. This marked the transition from their traditional nomadic lifestyle to more sedentary patterns. Today, most members of Ahl al-Sumada live in Saudi Arabia and Kuwait, where they have integrated into modern professional roles as professors, politicians, scholars, doctors, and engineers. Smaller populations can also be found in Iraq, Syria, Qatar, Bahrain, and Turkey, reflecting the historical migration patterns and modern diaspora of the Al-Dhafeer tribal confederation.

data.history: Al-Aslam is a tribal branch of the ancient Arabian tribe Banu Aslam, which played a significant role during the early Islamic period in the 7th century CE. According to tribal genealogies documented in the research, Aslam was descended from Haritha bin Amr, who led his branch of the Azd confederation from Yemen into the Hijaz and eventually to the Tihamah region. The lineage traces through Haritha bin Amr to his son Afsa, who became the father of Aslam, placing the tribe within the ancient Qahtanite (southern Arabian) tribal system rather than the Adnanite northern Arabian lineage. During the Prophet Muhammad's era, the Banu Aslam were active participants in the formative events of Islamic history. During the Invasion of Hamra al-Asad, Muhammad sent three spies from this tribe to track the departing Meccan Army, demonstrating their trusted position within the early Muslim community. The tribe formed part of a confederation that participated in the crucial Battle of the Trench (Battle of Khandaq), one of the defining conflicts of early Islam. Following Muhammad's death, the Banu Aslam proved their loyalty during the turbulent Ridda Wars by pledging allegiance to Abu Bakr, the first Caliph. Abu Bakr reportedly noted that upon seeing the Banu Aslam's support, he became certain of victory against the rebel tribes. The tribe produced prominent figures like Buraydah ibn al-Husayb, a companion of the Prophet Muhammad who hailed from the Sahm subdivision of the Aslam tribe. Buraydah participated in major early Islamic campaigns including the expedition to Hudhaybiyah, the Battle of Khaybar, and the conquest of Mecca, later settling in Basra and traveling throughout Khurasan. In the modern context, Al-Aslam appears as one of the three main branches of the Shammar tribal confederation, alongside Abdah and Sinjarah. According to 20th century demographic estimates, Al-Aslam comprised approximately 30% of the Shammar confederation by 1932, with the broader Shammar territory extending across the northern Arabian Peninsula from Ha'il.

data.history:
The Al Bu Sa'id Dynasty represents one of the most enduring ruling families in Arabian Peninsula history, tracing their origins to the Azd tribe through their patrilineal ancestor al-'Atik b. al-Asad b. Imran, who settled in Dibba (Dabá). Like other Qahtani tribes, the Azd originally migrated from Yemen following the destruction of the Marib Dam, establishing themselves as a leading force in early Islamic conquests under the celebrated general Al Muhallab ibn Abi Suffrah (Abu Said), the progenitor of the Busaid tribe. The dynasty's rise to power began with Ahmad bin Said al-Busaidi, born in Adam, Oman in 1694 to a small Hinawi tribe from the interior. Working his way up from reportedly being a wood vendor to becoming a leading merchant and governor of Sohar in 1737, Ahmad demonstrated exceptional military and political acumen during the Persian occupation under Nader Shah.

After enduring a nine-month siege of Sohar and negotiating terms with Persian forces, Ahmad gradually consolidated power across Oman. Following the defeat and death of rival claimant Bal'arab bin Himyar in 1749, Ahmad was elected Imam by the Ibadi tribes at Rustaq on June 9, 1749, marking the foundation of the Al Bu Sa'id dynasty. His 39-year reign established Oman as a major maritime power, maintaining permanent army and navy forces and encouraging agricultural and maritime trade development. The dynasty reached its zenith under Sultan Said bin Sultan (1804-1856), known as 'Said the Great' and the 'Lion of Oman.' After eliminating pretender Badr bin Saif in 1806 through a dramatic confrontation at Barka, Said expanded Omani influence from Iran to Zanzibar, establishing a vast maritime empire.

Said moved his capital to Zanzibar in 1832, initiated large-scale clove cultivation, and signed historic treaties with Britain (1798) and the United States (1833). Upon Said's death in 1856, the empire divided into two sultanates: Thuwaini bin Said ruling Muscat and Oman, while Majid bin Said governed Zanzibar. The Omani branch continued under British protection through various succession struggles, including the brief imamate of Azzan bin Qais (1868-1871) before power returned to the Al Bu Sa'id line under Turki bin Said. The dynasty modernized under Sultan Qaboos bin Said (1970-2020), who overthrew his father in a palace coup and ruled for 50 years, transforming Oman into a modern state while maintaining the family's 280-year rule. Today, Sultan Haitham bin Tariq Al Said continues the dynasty's reign, having been designated successor by Qaboos himself, with his son Theyazin bin Haitham named as the first Crown Prince in 2021.

data.history:
Al Jabr represents a section of the broader Na'im tribal confederation that settled in Qatar, with the Na'im tribe being traditionally classified as Ghafiri and widely distributed across the Arabian Peninsula. According to J.G. Lorimer's 1904 Gazetteer of the Persian Gulf, the Na'im were described as 'a Bedouin tribe who grazed their cattle on pastures surrounding Zubarah in 1873,' with 60-70 members of the tribe's Qatar branch having hereditary attachment to the Bahraini emir. The tribe was reported as being one of the most powerful tribes in Qatar in an 1890 British government report.

The Al Jabr section gained particular prominence during a significant tribal dispute in 1937 over Zubarah between Bahrain and Qatar. During this conflict, the Na'im in Qatar split into factions - the Ramzan section supported Qatari emir Abdullah bin Jassim Al Thani, while Al Jabr pledged allegiance to the ruler of Bahrain. In July 1937, tensions escalated when Sheikh Abdullah Al Thani moved against Na'im groups residing in Ath Thaqab over perceived violations of Qatar's laws. The Na'im leadership claimed Abdullah Al Thani had 'stolen their cattle' and burned their houses and villages.

The conflict reached its climax when Abdullah Al Thani sent an armed force numbering in the hundreds to confront around 60 armed Na'im men encamped in Ath Thaqab Fort. According to Rashid bin Mohammed, upon encountering the Qatari loyalists, he and his troops surrendered, but four of his men were killed anyway. Abdullah's forces captured the fort and occupied the villages of Ath Thaqab, Freiha, Al-ʽArish and Al Khuwayr. Following their defeat, Abdullah Al Thani confiscated 40 rifles from Ath Thaqab residents and livestock, leading to approximately 1,000 Na'im tribal members, including Al Jabr, going into exile in Bahrain.

Some Al Jabr members returned after the Second World War, re-establishing their presence in Qatar's tribal structure. The broader Na'im tribe, from which Al Jabr descends, is divided into three main sections: the Al Bu Kharaiban, the Khawatir, and the Al Bu Shamis. The traditional heart of Na'im territory was the oasis town of Buraimi and nearby Al Ain, where they followed the Ghafiri tradition and were involved in various conflicts with other tribes including the Bani Yas, Manasir, Bani Kaab, and Bani Qitab throughout their history.

data.history:
Al-Ma'alem (Arabic: المعاليم) is documented as a branch of the Al-Dhafeer tribe, which descends from Tayy Arabs through the genealogical line of Banu Lam. According to historical sources including Muhammad Amin al-Baghdadi's 'Gold Alloys in the Knowledge of Arab Tribes' and Hamad al-Jassir's 'Jamharat Ansab al-Osar in Najd', the Dhafeer genealogically came from Banu Lam and descended from Tayy. Al-Ma'alem forms part of Ahl al-Sumada, one of the two main divisions of Al-Dhafeer, alongside other branches including Al-Jawasim, Al-Thira'an, Al-Ma'adin, Al-Oraif, Al-'Aljanat, and Al-Askar.

The ancestral homes of the Al-Dhafeer tribe, and by extension Al-Ma'alem, can be traced back to areas near Medina, specifically a place called al-Dhaghn opposite Medina in the Hejaz region. The tribe initially lived in the desert east of Medina before migrating to central Najd, and later to the desert regions of north Najd and north Bahrain (Eastern Arabia). This territory encompassed the desert regions of south Iraq, north Saudi Arabia, and north Kuwait, where they lived as Bedouins without proper permanent settlements.

The Al-Dhafeer tribe, including the Al-Ma'alem branch, spread throughout Arabia through raiding and migration patterns typical of Bedouin tribes. They were nicknamed the 'People of flowing hair and camel caravans' due to their distinctive habit of conducting raids alongside women and children, demonstrating their nomadic lifestyle and tribal cohesion. The tribe participated in significant historical events, including providing cavalry and fighters to support the House of Khalifa against Persian rule in Bahrain during the Persian-Bahrain conflict of 1782.

Around 1926, a major transformation occurred when Emir Ibn Saud, after conquering large portions of Arabia, allowed the Saudi branch of the Al-Dhafeer tribe to settle in several permanent settlements after securing the roads. This marked the transition from their traditional nomadic lifestyle to settled communities. As a branch of Al-Dhafeer, Al-Ma'alem would have been part of these historical movements and settlements, maintaining their traditional social structure while becoming integrated into contemporary Gulf society, particularly in Saudi Arabia and Kuwait where most Al-Dhafeer tribal members now reside.

data.history: The research reveals no direct connection between the Al Omar tribal branch and any of the historical Omar/Umar figures mentioned in the sources. Omar al-Mukhtar (1858-1931) was a Libyan resistance leader from the Arab Mnifa tribe in Cyrenaica, who led native resistance against Italian colonization and belonged to the Senussi movement. Umar ibn al-Khattab (c. 584-644) was the second Rashidun caliph from the Banu Adi clan of Quraysh in Mecca, who played a crucial role in early Islamic expansion but has no documented genealogical connection to Arabian Peninsula tribal branches named Al Omar. The Bani Omar of Oman represents a separate tribal entity - they are descended from Maʿamar ibn Zubaid of the Zubaid tribe within the Madhhaj confederation, classified as Qahtanite lineage, and have traditionally inhabited Northern Oman in the wilayats of Liwa, Shinas, Saham, and Ibri. The existing data describing Al Omar as a Qahtani tribal branch within the Albu Salih confederation lacks verification in the provided research materials, which focus on unrelated historical figures and the distinct Omani Bani Omar tribe.

The House of Saud (Al Saud) traces its origins to Mani' ibn Rabiah Al-Muraydi, who settled in Diriyah in 1446-1447 with his clan, the Mrudah. The Mrudah are believed to be descended from the Banu Hanifa branch of the larger Rabi'ah tribal confederation, which forms part of the Adnani lineage. Mani' was invited to Diriyah by a relative named Ibn Dir, who handed him two estates called al-Mulaybeed and Ghusayba. The region was renamed Al Diriyah after their benefactor, and the Mrudah became rulers of this settlement along the banks of Wadi Hanifa. As the clan grew larger, power struggles ensued, with branches splitting off - one leaving for nearby Dhruma and another branch (the 'Al Watban') departing for az-Zubayr in southern Iraq. The Al Muqrin became the ruling family among the Mrudah in Diriyah, and the dynasty's name derives from Sheikh Saud ibn Muhammad ibn Muqrin, who died in 1725.

The First Saudi State was founded in 1727 by Muhammad bin Saud through a crucial alliance with the religious reformer Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab, establishing a strict Islamic polity that expanded across most of the Arabian Peninsula. This state reached its greatest extent under Saud ibn Abdulaziz, with raids extending into Yemen, Oman, Syria, and Iraq. The Saudis referred to themselves as the Muwahhidun or Ahl al-Tawhid ('the monotheists'), later called Wahhabis after their religious founder. The first state ended dramatically in 1818 when Ottoman-Egyptian forces destroyed Diriyah, executed the Saudi leadership, and sent family members into exile. The Second Saudi State emerged in 1824 under Turki ibn Abdallah with its capital in Riyadh, but was marked by severe internal conflicts and less territorial expansion, eventually falling to the Rashidis at the Battle of Mulayda in 1891.

The modern Saudi state began in 1902 when Abdulaziz ibn Saud (Ibn Saud) recaptured Riyadh's Masmak fort with 40 followers, beginning three decades of conquest that would unify most of the Arabian Peninsula. Through strategic alliances with the British, creation of the Ikhwan military-religious brotherhood, and systematic conquest, he defeated his main rivals: the Al Rashid clan (1921), the Ottomans in al-Hasa (1913), and the Sharifs of Mecca in the Hijaz (1925). Ibn Saud proclaimed the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia in 1932, and the discovery of oil in 1938 transformed the kingdom into a global power. The royal family comprises an estimated 15,000 members, with the ruling faction primarily descended from Ibn Saud, whose sons have ruled in succession: Saud (1953-1964), Faisal (1964-1975), Khalid (1975-1982), Fahd (1982-2005), Abdullah (2005-2015), and currently Salman (2015-present).

The House of Saud forms a subtribe of the prominent ancient Banu Hanifa tribe, from which the 7th century Arabian theologian Maslama ibn Ḥabīb originated. The family structure includes cadet branches descended from Muhammad bin Saud's brothers: the Al Jiluwi, Al Thunayan, Al Mishari, and Al Farhan branches, who hold high government positions but are not in the line of succession. Members often intermarry within the Al Saud to maintain lineage and influence. The succession system was traditionally based on agnatic seniority among Ibn Saud's sons, but was modified in 2006 to include election by a committee of princes. King Salman broke with tradition in 2017 by appointing his son Mohammed bin Salman as crown prince, marking the first father-to-son succession since Ibn Saud's death in 1953.
data.nameEtymology: The name 'Al Saud' is formed by adding the word 'Al' (meaning 'family of' or 'House of') to the personal name of the ancestor Saud ibn Muhammad ibn Muqrin, who died in 1725 and was the father of the dynasty's 18th century founder Muhammad bin Saud.

data.history: The research provided discusses the Ubaid period (c. 6500-3700 BCE), an archaeological prehistoric period in Mesopotamia named after Tell al-Ubaid, located west of the city of Ur in southern Iraq. This archaeological site gave its name to the Neolithic pottery and represents the first settlements in the alluvial plain of southern Mesopotamia. The Ubaid culture began around 5300 BCE and continued until the beginning of the Uruk period around 4000 BCE. The research makes no connection between this ancient archaeological culture and any modern tribal group called Al-Ubaid. The archaeological Ubaid culture was characterized by large unwalled village settlements, multi-room rectangular mud-brick houses, and the first public architectural temples in Mesopotamia. The society showed a two-tier settlement hierarchy with large central sites over 10 hectares surrounded by smaller village sites under one hectare. During this period, there was a trend toward urbanization with widespread agriculture and animal domestication practiced in settled communities. The culture originated in the south but shows clear connections to earlier cultures in central Iraq. The research extensively details the material culture, pottery, tools, burial practices, economy, and spread of this ancient civilization, but provides no information about any tribal group named Al-Ubaid in the Arabian Peninsula context.

The Almusaeada are documented as one of the Palestinian branches of the Mahamid tribe (Arabic: المحاميد), a significant Arab confederation that traces its genealogical origins to the Khawlaniyah al-Qahtaniyah Harb. This places them within the Qahtanite lineage, the southern Arabian tribal confederation distinct from the northern Adnanite Arabs. The Mahamid originally resided in Yemen and the Hijaz region between Mecca and Medina, establishing themselves as part of the broader Harb tribal system that dominated much of western Arabia.

According to tribal biographical accounts, when the Mahamid tribe grew extensively and their traditional territories in Yemen, Najd, and Hijaz became insufficient for their expanding population, the tribal leadership convened to determine their future course. Rather than engaging in warfare with neighboring tribes—many of whom were fellow Harb clans—they chose the path of migration. This decision reflects the complex inter-tribal relationships and the principle of avoiding conflict within the broader tribal confederation. The Mahamid subsequently embarked on a northward migration that would shape their historical trajectory.

The migration path took the Mahamid north to Dumat Al-Jandal, an important oasis settlement in the northern Arabian Peninsula, where they settled temporarily. From there, they continued their northward movement toward the Levant region. The tribe eventually reached the Karak region in present-day Jordan, where they established a significant presence. Their numbers were reportedly so substantial that they outnumbered the largest existing tribes in the region, demonstrating the scale of this migration and settlement.

In the Karak region, the Mahamid formed strategic alliances with local clans, most prominently with Al-Omaro Bani Uqba. Through these alliances and their numerical strength, they established what became known as the Emirate of Mahamid, with leadership primarily held by the Al-Amru clan. This emirate represented a significant political achievement, lasting for an extended period and demonstrating the Mahamid's successful adaptation to their new territorial base. However, historical accounts suggest that their rule eventually faced challenges, with some sources indicating that various clans united against them, leading to the emirate's eventual decline.

As the Mahamid presence expanded throughout the Levant, various branches emerged and settled in different regions. The Almusaeada represent one of the Palestinian branches that developed during this broader tribal dispersion. Along with other Palestinian branches including Kiawan, Alkhudur, Alsawalima, Alhamamada, Alhasasana, Aljeayisa, Dar Alearabi, Al'asead, and Al'ahmad, the Almusaeada became part of the Palestinian tribal landscape. This diversification reflects the common pattern of tribal segmentation that occurred as Arab tribes adapted to new territories and local conditions across the Levant region, maintaining their broader Mahamid identity while developing distinct local characteristics and territorial associations.

folkLegends.title: The Great Tribal Council
folkLegends.story: According to tribal biography, when the Mahamid tribe enlarged and their areas of residence in Yemen, Najd and Hijaz became too narrow, they gathered to take opinions about what they need to do. Some suggested conquering neighboring tribes, but this suggestion was rejected because most of those in the neighborhood were clans and sub-clans from the Harb tribe. All five gathered together, united by the fifth grandfather, and suggested leaving, settling their opinion on migration rather than warfare.

migrationPath.description: Initial northward migration from original territories in Yemen and Hijaz region to the northern Arabian oasis settlement
Continued migration toward the Levant, settling in the Karak region where they established the Emirate of Mahamid
Further dispersion of Mahamid branches into Palestine, where Almusaeada became one of the Palestinian branches

data.history:
The Anizah (عنزة) represents one of the largest and most influential Bedouin tribal confederations in the Arabian Peninsula, with origins predating the rise of Islam in the seventh century. Classical Arab genealogists place Anizah within the large Rabi'a branch of the Adnanites alongside Abd al-Qays, Banu Bakr, Bani Hanifa, and Taghlib tribes. Early historians recorded two main branches: nomadic groups in the northern Arabian steppes bordering Syria and Mesopotamia, and sedentary populations in the wadis of Al-Yamama in eastern Najd. According to al-Tabari, the Anizah joined with Bakr ibn Wa'il under an alliance called al-Lahazim, with many following Christianity prior to Islam. During the Rashidun period, 4,000 Anizah warriors joined Ali ibn Abi Talib's forces at the Battle of Siffin in 657 CE.

According to historians al-Hamdani and Abu'l-Hasan Bayhaqi, the Anizah settled in Khaybar at the end of the 10th century AD, expelling the descendants of Ja'far al-Tayyar from the region. The confederation gained prominence during the Ottoman era as masters of northwestern Arabian oasis towns, particularly Khaybar and Al-'Ula, collecting tribute known as 'khuwwa' from farmers in exchange for protection while migrating northward into southern Syria during summer months. The first Anizah group to enter Syria was the Hasana in the mid-18th century, establishing dominance around Hama and Homs, followed by the Wuld Ali in the Hawran region.

The early 19th century saw major Anizah expansion as the Ruwallah, Fad'an, and Sba'a entered Syrian plains, with the Ruwallah becoming so powerful around Al-Jouf and Wadi Sirhan that they practically developed into an independent tribe. In Najd, Anizah penetration began in the 14th century according to historian Ibn La'bun, with major settlement of Al-Qassim region in the 15th century. By the early 18th century, Anizah Bedouins reached as far as the gates of Riyadh, killing its ruler Zayd Abazara'a in battle. The 19th century witnessed Sheikh Mashaan Ibn Hathal leading Anizah clans to victory at the Battle of Al Shimasiyah on June 18, 1825, though he died in the battle.

The confederation's influence extends to modern Gulf ruling families, with the House of Khalifa of Bahrain and House of Sabah of Kuwait tracing ancestry to Anizah through the Bani Utbah alliance. The Al-Hathal family emerged as sheikhs of the Al-Hblan branch when their ancestor Hathal Ibn Adenan received the title 'Sheikh Al-Shuyoukh' from the Sharif of Makkah Saad Ibn Zeid (1666-1705) after successfully collecting zakat from a rebellious tribe. The establishment of modern Middle Eastern borders severely impacted their traditional nomadic lifestyle, forcing most to settle and adopt citizenship in various states while retaining tribal appellations like al-'Annizi or Al-Ruwaili.

folkLegends.title: Hathal Ibn Adenan and the Sharif's Zakat
folkLegends.story: Sheikh Hathal Ibn Adenan obtained leadership of Anizah when the Sharif of Makkah was unable to collect zakat from a rebellious tribe near Makkah. Hathal pledged to the Sharif to bring the zakat of this tribe, and when he successfully accomplished this mission, he was appointed as general sheikh of the tribe of Anizah and given the seal and bundle of Sheikhdom by Sharif Saad Ibn Zeid.

data.history: The research provided focuses exclusively on Abu Dawud al-Sijistani (202-275 AH), a prominent hadith scholar from the Azd tribe, rather than the broader Azd Uman confederation. Abu Dawud was born in Sijistan, a region neighboring Makran in Baloch territory, and belonged to the Azd tribal lineage through his ancestor Imran, who was killed at the Battle of Siffin fighting alongside Ali ibn Abi Talib. His full genealogy is recorded as Sulayman ibn al-Ash'ath ibn Ishaq ibn Bashir ibn Shaddad ibn Amr ibn Imran al-Azdi al-Sijistani, confirming his descent from the Azd, described as a well-known Yemeni tribe. The research notes that his grandfather Imran's participation in the Battle of Siffin (657 CE) demonstrates the Azd's early involvement in Islamic civil conflicts, positioning themselves with Ali's faction. Abu Dawud's scholarly journey took him across the Islamic world, from his birthplace in Sijistan through Khurasan, Iraq, Syria, and Egypt, before settling in Basra at the request of the Abbasid prince Abu Ahmad al-Muwaffaq. His compilation of hadith and his position as a leading Islamic scholar reflects the continued intellectual prominence of Azd descendants in the Islamic scholarly tradition during the 3rd century AH. The research confirms the Azd's Qahtani origins through its description as a Yemeni tribe, aligning with traditional genealogical classifications.

migrationPath.description: Abu Dawud's scholarly journey from Sijistan through various Islamic centers, ultimately settling in Basra to revive learning after the Zanj rebellion

data.history:
The Bani Shatair (Arabic: بني شطير) are one of two main sections of the Shihuh tribe, alongside the Bani Hadiyah, inhabiting the northern Hajar Mountain range in the UAE and northern Oman. As a tribal name, they originate in Ras Al Khaimah, UAE and areas of Northern Oman, forming part of the broader Shihuh tribal confederation that inhabits the Musandam Peninsula region.

The Bani Shatair are internally divided into subsections including the Kumzarah and Habus, reflecting the complex sub-tribal structure characteristic of Arabian Peninsula tribes. They practiced a traditional seasonal migration pattern, moving between different economic activities based on the agricultural and maritime calendar. During the pearling season, they would take work in Ras Al Khaimah and Rams along the coast, while during winter months they engaged in farming in the Ruus Al Jibal area of the Hajar Mountains.

The village of Sha'am, traditionally a dependency of Ras Al Khaimah, was originally settled entirely by the Bani Shatair, serving as one of their primary settlements. However, their relationship with the Rulers of Ras Al Khaimah was frequently contentious and fractious. This tension led to Sha'am seceding from Ras Al Khaimah several times throughout its history, reflecting the independent character of the Bani Shatair and their resistance to external control.

Historically, the Bani Shatair also inhabited the As Salamah Archipelago (known by the colonial name Quoin Islands) in the Persian Gulf, north of the Musandam Peninsula. These three uninhabited islands - Mumar Island (Great Quoin), Fanaku Island (Gap Island), and Didamar Island (Little Quoin) - were named using the Shihuh dialect. The Shihuh tribe, including the Bani Shatair, named the outer islands Mumar and Didamar, meaning 'mother' and 'daughter' respectively, and called the entire archipelago 'As Salamah wa Banatiha' meaning 'Salamah and her daughters.' The political resolution came in 1921 when Sha'am finally became part of the recognized Trucial State of Ras Al Khaimah, ending the cycle of secessions.

timelineEvents.title: Sha'am Integration
timelineEvents.description: The village of Sha'am, originally settled entirely by the Bani Shatair, became part of the recognized Trucial State of Ras Al Khaimah after several previous secessions

data.history:
Banu Jumah was one of the prominent clans of the Quraysh tribe in pre-Islamic Mecca, descended from Jumah ibn Amr ibn Husays ibn Ka'b ibn Lu'ayy ibn Ghalib ibn Fihr ibn Malik. They were closely related to the Banu Sahm clan, as both descended from Husays ibn Ka'b, forming the larger Banu Husays lineage within Quraysh. The clan was known for their wealth and commercial activities, establishing themselves as skilled merchants who played a significant role in Meccan trade networks connecting the Indian Ocean, East Africa, and the Mediterranean.

During the succession dispute following Qusayy ibn Kilab's death in the late 5th/early 6th century CE, Banu Jumah joined the al-Ahlaf ('the Confederates') faction alongside the Banu Sahm, Banu Adi, Banu Makhzum, and Banu Abd al-Dar clans, supporting Abd al-Dar against the claims of Abd Manaf. This factional division would influence Qurayshi politics for generations. The clan held significant religious responsibilities in pre-Islamic Mecca, including the Iysaar - the management of divination arrows used at the sacred idols.

Banu Jumah initially opposed Muhammad and Islam, with prominent clan leader Umayya ibn Khalaf serving as one of Islam's staunchest opponents. Umayya owned and tortured the early Muslim convert Bilal ibn Rabah, an Abyssinian slave who would later become Islam's first muezzin. Umayya was killed at the Battle of Badr in 624 CE, along with other notable clan members. His son Safwan ibn Umayya initially continued the family's opposition to Islam but eventually converted following the Muslim conquest of Mecca in 630 CE.

The clan produced several important early Muslim figures, including the Companion Uthman ibn Mad'hun, who was among the early converts to Islam and participated in the migration to Abyssinia. Other notable members included Umayr ibn Wahb, who initially plotted against Muhammad but later converted, and Said ibn Amir al-Jumahi, who commanded Muslim forces in Syria. According to Shia sources, a group from Banu Jumah participated in the Battle of the Camel in 656 CE alongside A'ishah against Ali ibn Abi Talib. Like other Qurayshi clans, their distinct tribal identity became less prominent as Islamic society developed, though they maintained their genealogical connections within the broader Qurayshi confederation.

data.history:
The Banu Makhzum was one of the most prominent and powerful clans of the Quraysh tribe in pre-Islamic Mecca, descended from Makhzum ibn Yaqaza. They formed part of the broader Adnani lineage through their Qurayshi ancestry, with the line of al-Mughira ibn Abd Allah ibn Umar ibn Makhzum emerging as the principal family within the clan. During the 6th century CE, they were aligned with the Ahlaf ('allies') faction alongside the clans of Abd al-Dar, Banu Sahm, Banu Jumah and Banu Adi, in rivalry to the Banu Hashim and Abd Shams factions within Quraysh tribal politics.

By the late 6th century, the Banu Makhzum had achieved extraordinary prominence in Meccan society, particularly under Hisham ibn al-Mughira, whose influence was so great that the Quraysh established a dating system beginning with his death. His family dominated the lucrative trade routes with Yemen and Ethiopia, making them one of the wealthiest clans in Mecca. This economic power translated into significant political influence within the Quraysh confederation.

The clan became the foremost opponents of Muhammad and early Islam in the early 7th century, with their chieftain Abu Jahl (Amr ibn Hisham) leading the opposition and organizing a devastating economic boycott of the Banu Hashim around 616-618 CE. Their hostility to Islam was so intense that they tortured early Muslim converts, including Ammar ibn Yasir and his family, who were under Makhzumite control. Abu Jahl personally murdered Sumayya, Ammar's mother, making her the first Muslim martyr.

The Battle of Badr in 624 CE marked a catastrophic turning point for the Banu Makhzum, where seven or eight nobles from the al-Mughira line were killed, severely weakening their power structure and causing them to be surpassed in prominence by the Banu Abd Shams. However, some members like Khalid ibn al-Walid eventually converted and joined Muhammad's forces during the conquest of Mecca in 630 CE. After Muhammad's death in 632 CE, former Makhzumite opponents became crucial military leaders in early Islamic expansion, with Khalid ibn al-Walid and Ikrima (son of Abu Jahl) playing pivotal roles in the Ridda wars and conquests in Syria and Iraq, though the forty male descendants of Khalid died in a plague during the late Umayyad period.

data.history: Banu Sahm was one of the prominent clans of the Quraysh tribe that controlled Mecca in pre-Islamic times. They descended from Sahm ibn Amr ibn Husays ibn Ka'b ibn Lu'ayy ibn Ghalib ibn Fihr ibn Malik, making them part of the Adnani lineage through Fihr ibn Malik, the progenitor of Quraysh. The clan was closely related to Banu Jumah, as both descended from the same ancestor within the Banu Husays grouping. According to traditional Arab genealogies, Fihr ibn Malik defended Mecca from the Himyarite Kingdom around 230-240 CE, leading forces that included the ancestors of Banu Sahm. Banu Sahm held significant religious and commercial responsibilities in Meccan society, controlling the sacred divination arrows (azlam) at the Kaaba and managing the organization of offerings and vows made to the idols. They were also responsible for settling disputes and conflicts within Meccan society. The clan formed part of the al-Ahlaf (Confederates) faction alongside Banu Abd al-Dar, Banu Adi, Banu Makhzum, and Banu Jumah, in political rivalry against the al-Mutayyabun faction that included Banu Hashim and other clans descended from Abd Manaf. This factional division emerged from succession disputes between Qusayy's natural successor Abd al-Dar and his chosen successor Abd Manaf in the late 5th/early 6th century. During the early Islamic period, Banu Sahm initially opposed Muhammad's message. However, several prominent members eventually converted to Islam and played crucial roles in the early Muslim conquests. Most notably, Amr ibn al-As al-Sahmi became one of Islam's greatest military commanders, leading the conquest of Egypt and serving as its first Muslim governor. The research mentions that during a commercial incident where a Yemenite merchant was robbed by al-As ibn Wa'il al-Sahmi, it led to the reformation of the Hilf al-Fudul alliance. Other distinguished members included Hisham ibn al-As, Abdullah ibn Amr ibn al-As (a renowned hadith narrator), and several others who participated in the second migration to Abyssinia in 615 CE to escape Meccan persecution. The clan's traditional power structure in Mecca was gradually transformed as the Islamic state expanded and political authority shifted from tribal confederations to the new Muslim administrative system.

data.history:
Banu Toghlib (also known as Taghlib ibn Wa'il) was a major pre-Islamic Arabian tribe that became part of the Al-Dawasir tribal confederation. According to Arabian genealogical tradition, Al-Dawasir originates from a confederation of tribes including the Azd and Banu Toghlib, connecting this ancient northern Arabian lineage to one of modern Arabia's most powerful tribal confederations. The Dawasir, incorporating the Banu Toghlib heritage, established their main base in Wadi Al-Dawasir in southern Najd, Saudi Arabia, and became one of the most influential tribes in the Arabian Peninsula.

The Dawasir tribe, carrying the Banu Toghlib lineage, achieved extraordinary political significance through their close ties with the Saudi royal family. The most important connection came through intermarriage, particularly between King Abdulaziz Al Saud and Hussa bint Ahmed Al Sudairi of the Sudairi family, a respected sub-clan of the Dawasir. She became King Abdulaziz's most influential wife and bore seven sons known as the Sudairi Seven, including future Kings Fahd and Salman. This maternal connection made the Dawasir the maternal uncles of much of the House of Saud family.

In Bahrain, the Dawasir established themselves as the second largest and most powerful tribe after the Utub, settling mainly in Zallaq and Budaiya and working extensively in the pearl industry. Their power was so significant that they recognized Sheikh Isa Al Khalifa as ruler 'in name only' and considered themselves immune from taxation. A major political crisis occurred in 1923 when the Dawasir opposed the British-supported transition of power from Sheikh Isa ibn Ali Al Khalifa to his son Hamad bin Isa Al Khalifa. Virtually all Dawasir left Bahrain for Dammam, Saudi Arabia, suspecting the new ruler would attempt to tighten control over them with British support.

The Dawasir were officially allowed to return to Bahrain in April 1927 after King Abdulaziz of Saudi Arabia requested Sheikh Hamad bin Isa to permit their return. Meanwhile, their departure led to the founding of important Saudi cities: Dammam and Khobar in Eastern Saudi Arabia were established in 1923 by the Dawasir migrants from Bahrain. The tribe initially settled in Khobar due to its proximity to Bahrain, hoping to return soon, but British obstacles forced them to later establish Dammam as their permanent settlement. The Dawasir presence extends across the Gulf, with historical settlements in Qatar where they influenced maritime borders, and in the UAE where they had a presence on Delma Island in Abu Dhabi, including notable pearl merchants like Fahad Bin Rashid Al Dosari.

data.history: Dar Alearabi is identified as a Palestinian branch of the larger Mahamid tribe, which traces its genealogical origins to the Khawlaniyah al-Qahtaniyah Harb confederation originally residing in Yemen and the Hijaz region between Mecca and Medina. The Mahamid's migration northward began when population growth constrained their original territories in the Arabian Peninsula. Rather than engaging in conquest against neighboring tribes, many of whom were fellow Harb clans, the tribe collectively decided on migration as their path forward. The Mahamid first established themselves in the Dumat Al-Jandal area before continuing their northward movement toward the Levant. They eventually settled in the Karak region, where they grew numerous enough to outnumber local tribes and formed strategic alliances with established Karak clans, particularly Al-Omaro Bani Uqba. From their position in the Karak region, the Mahamid established the Emirate of Mahamid, which was ruled primarily by the Al-Amru clan for an extended period, though historical sources indicate their governance became harsh enough to eventually unite other clans in opposition. From this Levantine base, various branches of the Mahamid dispersed throughout the region, with Dar Alearabi becoming one of several Palestinian branches alongside other groups including Kiawan, Alkhudur, and Alsawalima.
data.nameEtymology: Named after their ancestor Al-Arabi

The Drisah are a subsection of the larger Bani Kaab Arab tribal confederation that operates primarily in Oman and the United Arab Emirates. The Bani Kaab tribe is traditionally associated with areas around and north of the Omani Wilayat of Mahdah, and regions east of Buraimi including Wadi Kadra, Wadi Hatta, and Wadi Qor. The confederation consists of multiple subsections including the Drisah, Makatim, Misaid, Miyadilah, Miyalisah, Mizahamiyin, Nawaljiyin, Salalat, Sawalim, Shwaihiyin, Yidwah, and Zahairat.

Unlike other Bani Kaab subsections that had settled by the turn of the 20th century, the Drisah maintained their nomadic lifestyle along with the Shwaihiyin subsection. The broader Bani Kaab confederation numbered approximately 7,250 people, with 1,150 remaining Bedouin at this time. This distinction between settled and nomadic sections within the confederation reflects the varied adaptations to changing economic and political conditions in the region.

In 1844, the Drisah, as part of the Bani Kaab confederation, allied with Sheikh Khalifa bin Shakhbut Al Nahyan of the Bani Yas in a tribal confederation that successfully drove Wahhabi forces from Buraimi. This alliance demonstrated the fluid nature of tribal politics in southeastern Arabia, where confederations formed based on immediate strategic needs. However, these alliances proved temporary, as the Bani Kaab later aligned themselves with Saudi Arabia in the 1940s under Sheikh Obaid bin Jumah, who declared to the Governor of Saudi Arabia's Eastern Province that 'Our territories are yours.'

During the 1950s Buraimi Dispute, the Drisah supported the Saudi incursion under Sheikh Rashid bin Hamad of the Al Bu Shamis and engaged in combat against British forces and the Trucial Oman Levies. This conflict represented the culmination of decades of shifting tribal allegiances in the region, with the Drisah finding themselves opposed to their former Bani Yas allies. The dispute highlighted the complex interplay between traditional tribal territories, emerging oil interests, and the competing claims of regional powers in the formation of modern Gulf states.

data.history:
The Ghassanids were a Christian Arab tribal confederation that migrated from South Arabia to the Levant in the 3rd century CE as part of the Azd tribal confederation. According to Arab genealogical tradition, they descended from Jafna ibn Amr, who led his people northward following the collapse of the Marib Dam. Christian J. Robin's epigraphic studies suggest that between the 3rd and 4th centuries CE, the Ghassanids were the main tribal confederation acting in the Hejaz, with a base possibly stationed at Medina. The period of Ghassanid domination of this region ended when they migrated northwards into Syrian territory, which would remain their main base of operations until Islamic times.

After settling in the Levant, the Ghassanids became clients of the Byzantine Empire, serving as foederati responsible for defending the eastern frontier against Bedouin raids and rival powers. Their capital was established at Jabiyah in the Golan Heights, and their authority extended via tribal alliances with other Azdi tribes all the way to the northern Hijaz as far south as Yathrib (Medina). The Ghassanids frequently fought against the Lakhmid kingdom, a rival Arab polity allied with the Sasanian Empire, and played significant roles in the Roman-Persian conflicts of late antiquity.

The Ghassanids reached their zenith under al-Harith ibn Jabalah (529-569 CE), who received the highest imperial title ever bestowed upon a foreign ruler by Emperor Justinian I and was granted authority over all Arab allies of Byzantium. They were staunch Miaphysite Christians and played a crucial role in reviving the Syrian Miaphysite Church despite Orthodox Byzantine opposition. The confederation was temporarily dissolved under Emperor Maurice (582-585 CE) into its 15 member tribes due to religious and political tensions, but was restored when Maurice realized he could not defend the eastern frontier without Ghassanid assistance.

The dynasty's power ended with the Islamic conquests, suffering decisive defeat at the Battle of Yarmuk in 636 CE under their last phylarch Jabala ibn al-Ayham. After supposedly embracing Islam, Jabala left the faith and ultimately withdrew with his tribesmen to Byzantine-held Anatolia in 639. However, significant remnants remained in Syria, residing in Damascus and the Ghouta countryside. Many gradually converted to Islam and served the Umayyad Caliphate as governors, commanders, scribes, and court officials. During the Second Muslim Civil War in 684, the Ghassan supported continued Umayyad rule and helped nominate Marwan I as caliph during a summit at their old capital of Jabiyah. Two prominent Damascene Ghassanid scholarly families, those of Yahya ibn Yahya al-Ghassani and Abu Mushir al-Ghassani, achieved prominence in early Islamic Syria as hadith scholars and preservers of Syrian administrative history.

folkLegends.title: The Scattering of Azd
folkLegends.story: According to the 'Scattering of Azd' story, the Azd migrate northward from South Arabia and different groups of the tribe split off in different directions, with the Ghassan being one such group that eventually settled within the Roman limes. This migration was traditionally linked to the collapse of the Marib Dam.

data.nameEtymology: The Ghassanids were also known as the Jafnids, after their ancestor Jafna ibn Amr. The term Jafnids was preferred by medieval Arabic authors and modern scholars for the ruling stratum of Ghassanid society.

data.history:
The al-Hamsab (الهامزاب) are one of the Riverain Manasir clans (Manasir al-Nil) of Northern Sudan, inhabiting the Fourth Cataract region of the Nile in their homeland of Dar al-Manasir. They are part of the broader Manasir tribal confederation, which consists of both riverine and Bedouin divisions. The Manasir people trace their origins to a historical ancestor named Mansur, though multiple genealogical accounts exist within their oral tradition regarding this lineage.

According to Manasir oral traditions documented by their own relocation committee, several competing origin stories persist: some Manasir in Gezira claim descent from Mansur bin Qahtan of the Southern Arab Qahtaniyin, others trace their ancestry to Mansur bin 'Aim of the Kawahla tribe, while still others maintain they descend from the Shaiqi clan al-Hankab through Mansur as a descendant of King Sabir, having migrated after internal conflicts. Some contemporary oral tradition also connects Mansur to the lineage of al-'Abbas, the uncle of Prophet Muhammad.

Critical historical research suggests that the current Manasir community, including the al-Hamsab clan, represents a voluntary amalgamation over centuries between indigenous Nubian groups, Arab emigrants, and other tribal minorities. Early travelers documented Beja and Bisharin influences from the Red Sea Hills among the Manasir, and their burial customs show strong similarities to Nubian practices, indicating significant indigenous cultural continuity despite Arab genealogical claims.

The al-Hamsab, like other Riverain Manasir clans, pursue small-scale agriculture on alluvial soils near the Nile, with date cultivation as their primary cash crop sold on national markets. They are also known for their skill in building traditional mud houses (Galus). Their population, along with all other Dar al-Manasir villages, was significantly affected by the Merowe Dam project, which necessitated relocation from their traditional homeland along the Fourth Cataract.

folkLegends.title: Descent from Mansur bin Qahtan
folkLegends.story: The Manasir living in Gezira claim their apical forefather is Mansur bin Qahtan, belonging to the Southern Arabs called Qahtaniyin
folkLegends.source: Manasir oral tradition documented by relocation committee

folkLegends.title: Kawahla Origin Theory
folkLegends.story: Some historians trace the origin of the Manasir back to the Kawahla, claiming they are sons of Mansur bin 'Aim

folkLegends.title: Shaiqi Migration Legend
folkLegends.story: Other historians claim the Manasir descend from cousins of the Shaiqi clan al-Hankab who migrated after internal fights, with Mansur being a direct descendant of King Sabir
